summary: We are testing whether a tight-trust predictor (±2 error bound) can find the optimal MaxBatchSize in ≤5 oracle calls with zero throughput gap, Pareto-dominating pure binary search (9 calls) while confirming that the ratio-based refinement step is necessary to eliminate gap. This is iteration 4, building on validated predictor accuracy (max_error=2) from iteration 3.
key_points: Four arms map the Pareto front of (oracle calls, throughput gap): h-main (predictor_direct, ≤5 calls, gap=0), h-control-negative (binary search, 9 calls, gap=0), h-ablation (predictor without refinement, 3 calls, gap>0 for 2/4 scenarios), h-robustness (existing ±5 hybrid, 5 calls, gap=0 but higher mean calls).

Confirmation criteria: predictor_direct achieves gap_throughput_rel=0 across all 4 scenarios with worst-case ≤5 calls; predictor_naive shows measurable gap (~2.5% baseline, ~0.5% long-loose-itl) proving refinement is load-bearing; ratio_binary_search uses exactly 9 calls establishing the no-predictor cost baseline.

Refutation signals: if predictor_direct exceeds 5 calls or produces gap>0, the ±2 error bound from iteration 3 was not reliable; if predictor_naive shows gap=0, the predictor error was actually 0 (making refinement unnecessary); if predictor_hybrid fails but predictor_direct succeeds, something is inconsistent in the oracle model.
